context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really confused about this 700–800 word reflection on global poverty. The prompt feels like it’s asking about poverty, microfinance, development theory, globalization, and MDGs all at once, and I can’t tell what the main argument is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. But before we untangle it, let me ask you this: what do all those topics (poverty alleviation, microfinance, development theory, globalization, MDGs) seem to have in common? What bigger question might they all be circling around?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should guide them to see that the parts are connected and ask them to identify the thread themselves. This uses guided discovery and keeps scaffolding minimal.

exchanges.student: I guess maybe they’re all about whether “development” really helps poor people or just says it does? I’m not sure if that’s too vague though, and then I immediately get lost again on where microfinance fits.
exchanges.tutor: That instinct is actually a strong starting point — keep it. Now, with that lens in mind: what do you know about microfinance? What is it supposed to do for poor people, and who typically promotes it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a promising central thread — questioning whether development delivers on its promises to the poor. I should affirm the direction without over-praising, then use a Socratic question to help them connect microfinance to that thread themselves, rather than explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: I only know the really basic version, like small loans for poor people, especially women, to start little businesses. But then I don’t know if I’m supposed to treat that as a real development strategy, a poverty band-aid, or like a symptom of a bigger system problem.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've actually just outlined three different theoretical positions on microfinance. Now, the prompt specifically asks you to evaluate it 'in terms of theories of economic development.' So let me ask: what development theories have you encountered in your readings so far? How do they think about what causes poverty in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified three possible framings for microfinance (real strategy, band-aid, symptom) which is good analytical thinking. Rather than telling them which framing to use, I should push them to connect these framings to the theories of economic development mentioned in the prompt, which is what the assignment actually asks. This keeps the discovery guided and moves toward the solution.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck because I kind of remember broad categories but not cleanly. I keep mixing up modernization, dependency, neoliberal stuff, and maybe participatory development, and they all blur together in my head.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need all four perfectly sorted out for this reflection. Pick just one or two that feel most relevant and tell me: according to that theory, what *causes* poverty? That question will help you figure out where microfinance fits.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on theory distinctions. This is a real obstacle but I shouldn't lecture them on all the theories. I should help them focus on just enough to move forward — asking them to pick one or two and think about what each says about the *cause* of poverty, since that's what connects theories to the microfinance question. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think modernization would say poverty is from being “underdeveloped” and needing better markets, institutions, maybe entrepreneurship? And dependency would say poverty comes from how the global system keeps poorer countries subordinate, but then I’m not sure if that means microfinance is good, useless, or actually distracting.
exchanges.tutor: You're very close to something important there. If dependency theory says poverty comes from structural global inequalities, what does that imply about a solution that focuses on giving individual poor people small loans? Does it address the cause?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid contrast between modernization and dependency theory and is naturally arriving at the tension the prompt is pointing toward. I should affirm the direction and push them to complete the logical step themselves — what does each theory imply about microfinance as a solution? This moves directly toward the core argument of their reflection.

exchanges.student: I mean… I guess not really? It sounds like it helps individuals cope, but not the structure itself. But then I get confused because if it helps even a little, am I supposed to criticize it anyway, or say it’s limited but still useful?
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling is actually the interesting part — you don't have to pick a side cleanly. But here's a sharper question: can a tool that helps individuals cope with poverty ever *reduce* poverty at a systemic level? What does your answer imply about the limits of poverty-focused development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a false binary — criticize vs. defend microfinance. This is actually a sophisticated analytical moment and I should push them to see that the reflection doesn't require a verdict but rather a nuanced position. I'll ask them to think about what 'limited but useful' actually means theoretically, which connects back to the prompt's question about hyper-capitalism.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly where my brain short-circuits. I feel like the answer is “sometimes maybe,” but then that sounds weak, and I still don’t know how to connect that to the hyper-capitalism part without turning it into a giant abstract rant.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have more than enough to start writing. You have a central question, two theoretical lenses, and a real tension about microfinance. Rather than solving the hyper-capitalism connection in the abstract, try drafting your opening paragraph — just state what the reflection is about and what tension it explores. What you write might surprise you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same tension and showing some frustration. We've now covered enough ground — theories, microfinance critique, structural vs. individual solutions — that they should attempt to synthesize this into writing rather than continuing to think out loud. Per the rules on redundancy and frustration, I should push them toward a written attempt rather than keep discussing abstractly.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the opening too. Could you just explain how the hyper-capitalism part is supposed to connect to poverty focus and microfinance, because that part still feels weirdly huge and I think I need the whole picture before I can write anything.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you that explanation — and honestly, waiting for the whole picture before writing is what's keeping you stuck. You already said microfinance helps individuals but not the structure. Try this: what kind of economic system would *prefer* individual solutions over structural ones? You already know more than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the connection directly, which would mean giving them the answer. I should refuse this while acknowledging their anxiety. I should also push back on the idea that they need the 'whole picture' before writing — this is a common avoidance pattern. I'll redirect them to what they already know and encourage a low-stakes first attempt.

exchanges.student: I guess a hyper-capitalist system would prefer market-based, individual fixes because they don’t challenge bigger inequalities. But then I start wondering if the whole essay is just “poverty gets individualized,” and I can’t tell if that’s enough or too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid analytical thread, not a simplistic one. But your reflection also needs to address how poverty alleviation became central to development *in the first place*, and whether tools like MDGs measure poverty satisfactorily. Do those questions complicate or reinforce the argument you just made?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially arrived at a strong central argument. I should acknowledge this without over-praising, and push back on the 'too simplistic' worry by redirecting them to the other parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet (MDGs, measurement, how development came to be associated with poverty alleviation). This also addresses potential redundancy — we've been on microfinance/hyper-capitalism for several exchanges. Time to broaden.

exchanges.student: They probably reinforce it, but I’m not fully seeing how. I think maybe once development gets defined as poverty reduction, then things like MDGs make it look manageable with targets and numbers, but I’m not sure if that’s the kind of point I’m allowed to make.
exchanges.tutor: You are absolutely allowed to make that point — and it's a good one. Now look at what you've built: development gets redefined as poverty reduction, individual solutions like microfinance fit neatly into that frame, MDGs make it measurable and manageable, and none of it challenges the structural system. That's your argument. Stop waiting for permission and start your draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely insightful connection between the definition of development, measurement tools like MDGs, and the broader argument about individualization/technocratization of poverty. They're also asking for permission to make the argument, which is a confidence issue I should address firmly but encouragingly. We are now at exchange 10, so I should push them strongly toward writing their reflection, integrating everything discussed.
